tossups.question: In a play by this author, a man’s wife threatens to fire a maid as she puts pillows in his armchair, upon which the maid hits him in the head with another pillow. That maid created by this author repeats “ignoramus!” as her master lists foods he was asked to eat, while she goes in and out of disguise between entrances. In an adaptation of Aulularia by this author, a valet helps negotiate an unfair loan that is secretly from his master’s father to help (*) Mariane; that valet later digs up and steals the father’s beloved cash box. In a play by this author, the maid Toinette has her master play dead, revealing that his wife Béline does not love him; earlier, she dresses up as a doctor to fool this author’s supposedly sickly protagonist Argan. For 10 points, name this playwright of The Miser and The Imaginary Invalid.
tossups.question_sanitized: In a play by this author, a man's wife threatens to fire a maid as she puts pillows in his armchair, upon which the maid hits him in the head with another pillow. That maid created by this author repeats "ignoramus!" as her master lists foods he was asked to eat, while she goes in and out of disguise between entrances. In an adaptation of Aulularia by this author, a valet helps negotiate an unfair loan that is secretly from his master's father to help (*) Mariane; that valet later digs up and steals the father's beloved cash box. In a play by this author, the maid Toinette has her master play dead, revealing that his wife Beline does not love him; earlier, she dresses up as a doctor to fool this author's supposedly sickly protagonist Argan. For 10 points, name this playwright of The Miser and The Imaginary Invalid.

tossups.question: In response to criticism of one of his plays, this author wrote another play in which the critics themselves watch that play. In another of this man’s plays, the protagonist insults a sonnet written by another character, which leads to that protagonist being charged in court. In that play by this author, Alceste attempts to woo a young woman despite being the titular (*) misanthrope. In another play, this author writes about Orgon hiding under a table as the title character seduces his wife. For 10 points, identify this French playwright who wrote about an impostor in his play Tartuffe.
tossups.answer: Molière [accept Jean-Baptiste Poquelin] (The first play is The School for Wives, which Molière later included in the play Critique of The School for Wives. The second and third lines refer to The Misanthrope.)
tossups.answer_sanitized: Moliere [accept Jean-Baptiste Poquelin] (The first play is The School for Wives, which Moliere later included in the play Critique of The School for Wives. The second and third lines refer to The Misanthrope.)
tossups.question_sanitized: In response to criticism of one of his plays, this author wrote another play in which the critics themselves watch that play. In another of this man's plays, the protagonist insults a sonnet written by another character, which leads to that protagonist being charged in court. In that play by this author, Alceste attempts to woo a young woman despite being the titular (*) misanthrope. In another play, this author writes about Orgon hiding under a table as the title character seduces his wife. For 10 points, identify this French playwright who wrote about an impostor in his play Tartuffe.

tossups.question: In one play, this character decides upon a change in profession after proclaiming that “a shoe maker in making shoes cannot spoil a scrap of leather without paying for it.” After four doctors cannot decide on the best treatment for this character’s daughter, he attempts to search for a panacea called Orvietan. This character is ordered by his master to invite a stone statue of “the Commander” in the forest to a dinner. In one play, this character accidentally picks up a miniature portrait of Lélie after attempting to help the fainted Valère. In The Feast of Stone, this character appears as the servant to Don Juan. This character alternately titles the play The (*) Imaginary Cuckold. While working as a woodcutter, this character is beaten and forced to heal Lucinde in The Doctor in Spite of Himself. The first non-anonymous play to depict the trope of the “flying doctor” casts him as this character. For 10 points, name this recurring Molière character often portrayed by the author himself.
tossups.answer: Sganarelle [prompt on Molière until mentioned with “what character did he often play?”]
tossups.answer_sanitized: Sganarelle [prompt on Moliere until mentioned with "what character did he often play?"]
tossups.question_sanitized: In one play, this character decides upon a change in profession after proclaiming that "a shoe maker in making shoes cannot spoil a scrap of leather without paying for it." After four doctors cannot decide on the best treatment for this character's daughter, he attempts to search for a panacea called Orvietan. This character is ordered by his master to invite a stone statue of "the Commander" in the forest to a dinner. In one play, this character accidentally picks up a miniature portrait of Lelie after attempting to help the fainted Valere. In The Feast of Stone, this character appears as the servant to Don Juan. This character alternately titles the play The (*) Imaginary Cuckold. While working as a woodcutter, this character is beaten and forced to heal Lucinde in The Doctor in Spite of Himself. The first non-anonymous play to depict the trope of the "flying doctor" casts him as this character. For 10 points, name this recurring Moliere character often portrayed by the author himself.

tossups.question: Ariane Mnouchkine’s (“noosh-keen’s”) biopic (“bio-pic”) of this playwright draws on an “impromptu” one-act play of a stage rehearsal that was written as a response to critics. The multi-day performance Pleasures of the Enchanted Island featured the scandalous premiere of a play by this co-founder of the Illustrious Theater Company that was later translated into English by the poet Richard Wilbur. In a play by this author, the Music Master and the Dancing Master stage (*) ballet scenes for their student, the social-climbing aristocrat Jourdain (“zhoor-DAN”). This playwright suffered a fatal tubercular coughing fit while starring in one of his plays as a hypochondriac. One of this author’s plays ends with a deus ex machina in which his royal patron arrests the title scheming religious hypocrite. For 10 points, name this French comic playwright of Tartuffe.
tossups.answer: Molière (“mole-YAIR”) [or Jean-Baptiste Poquelin] (The first line refers to Ariane Mnouchkine’s Molière and Molière’s The Impromptu of Versailles.)
tossups.answer_sanitized: Moliere ("mole-YAIR") [or Jean-Baptiste Poquelin] (The first line refers to Ariane Mnouchkine's Moliere and Moliere's The Impromptu of Versailles.)

In a play by this author, the Music Master and the Dancing Master stage (*) ballet scenes for their student, the social-climbing aristocrat Jourdain ("zhoor-DAN"). This playwright suffered a fatal tubercular coughing fit while starring in one of his plays as a hypochondriac. One of this author's plays ends with a deus ex machina in which his royal patron arrests the title scheming religious hypocrite. For 10 points, name this French comic playwright of Tartuffe.

tossups.question: The film Hiroshima mon amour [mahn ah-MOOR] is analyzed as a remake of this image in a book by Giuliana Bruno that points out this image’s resemblance to the female reproductive tract. In a play, Cathos mocks men who don’t know about “sprightly verses” and “polite epistles” because of their ignorance of this image. This image, which is often interpreted as a representation of a feminist utopia, includes a depiction of “new (*) friendship” in the bottom center. This image, which was produced in the 1650s by Madame de Rambouillet [rom-bwee-YAY] and other members of the précieuses [pray-see-UZZ], was incorporated into the novel Clélie [clay-LEE] by Mademoiselle de Scudéry [skoo-day-REE]. For 10 points, name this image showing locations like the town of Respect, the River of Inclination, and the Lake of Indifference.
tossups.answer: Carte de Tendre [or Carte du Pays de Tendre; accept Map of the Country of Tendre; accept map of tenderness/love/romance/feelings/emotions/relationships/any closely related concept] (The Giuliana Bruno book is Atlas of Emotion, and the play is Molière’s The Pretentious Young Ladies.)
tossups.answer_sanitized: Carte de Tendre [or Carte du Pays de Tendre; accept Map of the Country of Tendre; accept map of tenderness/love/romance/feelings/emotions/relationships/any closely related concept] (The Giuliana Bruno book is Atlas of Emotion, and the play is Moliere's The Pretentious Young Ladies.)

For 10 points, name this image showing locations like the town of Respect, the River of Inclination, and the Lake of Indifference.

tossups.question: One of this author's characters curses "novels, verses, songs," and "sonnets" as "pernicious amusements of idle minds" at the end of the play in which he appears. That character, Gorgibus, vows to marry off his daughter and niece or send the two to a nunnery. In another play by this author, a maid voices her opinion of a forced marriage but stops talking every time her master is about to slap her. In that play, two lovers repeatedly walk away from each other as Dorine tries to mend their relationship, which was ruined by Mariane's arranged marriage. One of this man's title characters has his licentiousness revealed to Orgon as Orgon hides under a table. For 10 points, name this playwright who satirized French society in his comedies of manners The Affected Young Ladies and Tartuffe.
tossups.question_sanitized: One of this author's characters curses "novels, verses, songs," and "sonnets" as "pernicious amusements of idle minds" at the end of the play in which he appears. In another play by this author, a maid voices her opinion of a forced marriage but stops talking every time her master is about to slap her. In that play, two lovers repeatedly walk away from each other as Dorine tries to mend their relationship, which was ruined by Mariane's arranged marriage. One of this man's title characters has his licentiousness revealed to Orgon as Orgon hides under a table. For 10 points, name this playwright who satirized French society in his comedies of manners The Affected Young Ladies and Tartuffe.

tossups.question: In one play by this author, the high-class Angelica marries the lower-class title character, who has to put up with her caprices. This author of George Dandin wrote a play whose title character has a name which derives from the Latin word for a grappling hook. Another of his plays includes the quack doctor Thomas Diafoirus, who is in love with Angélique, the daughter of the title character. In one of his plays, Célimène is courted by several men, including Alceste, despite the latter’s indisguised hatred for most people. He wrote a play in which Valère tries to win the heart of Mariane, the daughter of Orgon, while the title religious hypocrite tries to stop him. For 10 points, name this author of The Miser, The Misanthrope, The Imaginary Invalid and Tartuffe.

For 10 points, name this author of The Miser, The Misanthrope, The Imaginary Invalid and Tartuffe.

The title doctor convinces the villagers of Saint-Maurice that they are suffering from an obscure illness in Knock, a play by this French author of the 8,000-page Men of Good Will, a novel written in a style he called "unanimism."

bonuses.leadin: This character sardonically observes that while craftsmen are forced to pay for material they destroy, those in his new profession can pretty much butcher whomever they want. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this character whose wife Martine gets revenge on him by claiming he is an amazing doctor, leading Géronte’s servants to attack him while he is drinking and chopping wood in a forest.
Sganarelle, the titular Doctor in Spite of Himself, was created by this French dramatist. He died hours after playing the hypochondriac Argan from one of his plays.

An essay dictated to this person discusses the “abstract musical energy” the author experienced while rehearsing The Doctor In Spite Of Himself. This dramatist translated several Molière plays and Hermann Suderman’s Teja into her local dialect, eschewing standard English.
answers: Sganarelle [prompt on the doctor in spite of himself]
Lady Gregory [accept Isabella Augusta Gregory; accept Isabella Augusta Persse; prompt on Gregory]
answers_sanitized: Sganarelle [prompt on the doctor in spite of himself]
Lady Gregory [accept Isabella Augusta Gregory; accept Isabella Augusta Persse; prompt on Gregory]

bonuses.leadin: This writer’s novel Clélie (klay-LEE) introduced the carte de tendre (kart duh TON-druh), a map depicting the path towards love. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this French writer who coined the term roman à clef (ro-MAWN ah KLAY) and fictionalized the lives of 17th century socialites in novels such as Artamène (ar-tah-MEN), which is among the longest novels ever written.
De Scudéry was among the witty précieuses (prace-YUZZ) mocked in this playwright’s The Ridiculous Précieuses. Orgon falls under the spell of a hypocrite in his play Tartuffe.
De Scudéry solves the mystery surrounding the death of the renowned goldsmith René Cardillac (ruh-NAY kar-dee-YAK) in a novella by this Prussian writer, whose “tales” include “The Sandman.”
answers: E. T. A. Hoffmann [or Ernst Theodor Amadeus Hoffmann]
answers_sanitized: E. T. A. Hoffmann [or Ernst Theodor Amadeus Hoffmann]

bonuses.leadin: The conservative senator Baron Courtin embezzles money from a charitable home for Catholic girls in this author’s play Le Foyer. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this French man of letters who detailed the experiences of the servant Célestine in his Diary of a Chambermaid. He portrayed the greedy businessman Isidore Lechat in his 1903 play Business is Business.
Mirbeau’s drama falls mostly into the “comedy of manners” style of this earlier French playwright of The Misanthrope and Tartuffe.

As the literary critic at Le Figaro, Mirbeau’s praise of this Belgian symbolist playwright’s 1890 work Princess Maleine was instrumental in launching this man’s career. He also wrote Pelléas and Mélisande.

bonuses.leadin: In this play, a girl and her lover, who is disguised as a music master, sing a duet about shepherds which is a thinly veiled description of the play’s actual plot. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this play in which Angelique marries Cleante despite being promised to a doctor by her hypochondriac father Argan.
The Imaginary Invalid was written by this 17th-century French author of comedies such as The Misanthrope and Tartuffe.
The maid Toinette and Argan’s brother Beralde get Argan to do this to find that his wife Beline does not actually love him. He later does this to trick Angelique and Cleante.
answers: fakes his own death [accept reasonable synonyms, prompt on “hiding” or “spying” since he does those things as well]
answers_sanitized: The Imaginary Invalid [or Le Malade Imaginaire]
fakes his own death [accept reasonable synonyms, prompt on "hiding" or "spying" since he does those things as well]

Oronte sues Alceste after Alceste does this to him. Descriptive

bonuses.leadin: In John Vanbrugh’s The Relapse, Colley Cibber played a lord who is named after this type of character in English drama. For 10 points each:
answers: fop (Lord Foppington appears in The Relapse, and Sir Fopling Flutter appears in The Man of Mode.)
parts: Name this character type that also names the protagonist of Etherege’s The Man of Mode. It refers to a man who is overly concerned with his appearance.

bonuses.leadin: A character in this play says he doesn’t know where hamsters belong while defending his choice to let Nibbles run away on the sidewalk. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this play, in which two couples meet to discuss a playground altercation between their children and devolve into savagery. A turning point occurs when Annette vomits over previously praised coffee table books.
God of Carnage was written by this contemporary French playwright. Three friends turn against each other after one of them spends a fortune on a modern white painting in this author’s play ‘Art’.
Reza’s plays have won several French theatre awards named for this comic playwright of The Miser and The School for Wives.
answers_sanitized: God of Carnage [or Le Dieu du Carnage]

bonuses.leadin: Name the following about 17th century French drama, for 10 points each.
parts: One of the few comedians, this playwright of The School for Wives and The Hypochondriac wrote about the alcoholic woodcutter Sganarelle in The Doctor Despite Himself.

This other dramatist was known for his adaptations of ancient or Classical works; he re-told the story of the Trojan War in Andromaque, while Voltaire called his Athalie the greatest triumph of the human mind.

In this play by Pierre Corneille, the titular martyr’s wife Pauline unsuccessfully tries to convince him to renounce his Christianity. The protagonist entrusts her to the Roman soldier Servetus before he is put to death.

bonuses.leadin: For 10 points each, name the following French dramatists.
parts: This comedian produced Tartuffe and The Imaginary Invalid; he died during a production of that latter play.

This writer wrote a trilogy that includes Begearss, a scheming Irishman that tries to fleece others, like Count Almaviva. That trilogy consists of The Guilty Mother, The Marriage of Figaro and The Barber of Seville.

This author converted to Jansenism and spent the last years of his life writing the Spiritual Canticles. His plays include one about a sadistic Nero, who poisons the title character and drives off Junie into a convent.

bonuses.leadin: This playwright was often in the favor of the Royal Court but his satire angered the Catholic Church. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this French comic playwright who ironically died after coughing up blood on stage while acting in his play, The Imaginary Invalid.
This Molière play is about Arnolphe, a man so ignorant of women that he attempts to raise Agnes to be his wife from birth.

This Molière play, which includes musical interludes, tells of Mr. Jourdain, the son of cloth salesman, who attempts to become powerful by marrying his daughter, Lucille, to Cleonte who is disguised as a Turkish prince.
answers: Le Bourgeois gentilhomme [or The Bourgeois Gentleman or The Middle-Class Aristocrat or The Would-Be Noble]
answers_sanitized: L'Ecole des Femmes [or The School for Wives]
Le Bourgeois gentilhomme [or The Bourgeois Gentleman or The Middle-Class Aristocrat or The Would-Be Noble]

This Moliere play is about Arnolphe, a man so ignorant of women that he attempts to raise Agnes to be his wife from birth.

This Moliere play, which includes musical interludes, tells of Mr. Jourdain, the son of cloth salesman, who attempts to become powerful by marrying his daughter, Lucille, to Cleonte who is disguised as a Turkish prince.

bonuses.leadin: In this play, Mascarille keeps reciting a "funny poem" he wrote with the punchline, "Stop thief! Stop thief!" For ten points each:
parts: Name this satire in which Cathos and Magdelon put on airs of sophistication for their debut in Paris.
This playwright of The Pretentious Young Ladies would have played Mascarille in the first few productions. He also wrote Tartuffe and The Misanthrope.
In this other play, Moliere would have played the pompous Arnolphe, who tries to raise Agnès for his wife without exposing her to the ways of the world.
answers: The Pretentious Young Ladies [or The Affected Young Ladies, or Les Précieuses ridicules]
answers_sanitized: The Pretentious Young Ladies [or The Affected Young Ladies, or Les Precieuses ridicules]
The School for Wives [or L'Ecole des Femmes]
